# Clawq MS Teams Integration

> Self-knowledge reference for the MS Teams channel in clawq. Use this to configure, troubleshoot, and understand the Teams Bot Framework integration.

## Overview

Clawq connects to Microsoft Teams via the **Microsoft Bot Framework** webhook protocol. Teams POSTs JSON activity payloads to your configured webhook URL; clawq processes the message asynchronously and replies via the Bot Framework REST API using an OAuth 2.0 bearer token obtained from Azure AD.

No long-polling or persistent WebSocket is needed — the channel is fully webhook-driven.

## Configuration

Add to `~/.clawq/config.json`:

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "teams": {
      "app_id": "your-azure-application-id",
      "app_secret": "your-azure-client-secret",
      "tenant_id": "your-azure-directory-tenant-id",
      "webhook_path": "/teams/webhook",
      "service_url": "https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer",
      "allow_teams": ["*"],
      "allow_users": ["*"]
    }
  }
}
```

Or via CLI:

```bash
clawq config set channels.teams.app_id "your-app-id"
clawq config set channels.teams.app_secret "your-secret"
clawq config set channels.teams.tenant_id "your-tenant-id"
```

### Config Fields and Defaults

| Field | Default | Description |
|-------|---------|-------------|
| `app_id` | (required) | Azure AD Application (client) ID |
| `app_secret` | (required) | Azure AD client secret |
| `tenant_id` | (required) | Azure AD Directory (tenant) ID |
| `webhook_path` | `/teams/webhook` | Inbound webhook path on the clawq gateway |
| `service_url` | `https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer` | Bot Framework service base URL (per-activity `serviceUrl` takes precedence at runtime) |
| `allow_teams` | `["*"]` | Allowed team IDs; `["*"]` allows all teams |
| `allow_users` | `["*"]` | Allowed user AAD object IDs; `["*"]` allows all users |

`app_id`, `app_secret`, and `tenant_id` are required; the channel is disabled if any of these are missing.

## Azure Setup Steps

### Step 0 — Get your public HTTPS URL first

You need an HTTPS URL before setting up Azure. Azure rejects HTTP.

Start clawq with a tunnel to get a URL:
  clawq config set tunnel.enabled true && clawq agent
Note the tunnel URL (e.g. https://abc123.trycloudflare.com).

### Step 1 — Register an Azure AD application

Docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app

1. Azure Portal → App registrations → New registration.
2. Note Application (client) ID → app_id.
3. Note Directory (tenant) ID → tenant_id.
4. Certificates & secrets → New client secret → app_secret.
   Copy the value immediately; it is only shown once.

Warning: Client secrets expire (12–24 months default). Rotate before expiry.
When expired, clawq logs "Teams: token fetch failed" and replies stop.

### Step 2 — Create an Azure Bot

Docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bot-service/bot-service-quickstart-registration

1. Azure Bot Service → Create Bot resource.
2. Set Messaging Endpoint: https://your-tunnel-url/teams/webhook (MUST be HTTPS).
3. Under Microsoft App ID: use existing app registration from Step 1.
4. In the Bot resource, Channels blade → Add Microsoft Teams.

### Step 3 — Create a Teams App Manifest (REQUIRED — most common missed step)

Docs: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoftteams/platform/bots/how-to/create-a-bot-for-teams

Without this step, the bot is registered in Azure but completely invisible
inside Teams. You cannot message a bot that has no Teams app.

1. Go to Teams Developer Portal: https://dev.teams.microsoft.com
2. Apps → New app. Fill in name and description.
3. App features → Bot → Enter a bot ID (your Application client ID from Step 1).
4. Set scope: Personal and/or Team.
5. Publish to your org or sideload for testing.
6. In Teams: Apps → find your app → Add.

### Step 4 — Configure clawq

  clawq config set channels.teams.app_id "your-app-id"
  clawq config set channels.teams.app_secret "your-secret"
  clawq config set channels.teams.tenant_id "your-tenant-id"

### Step 5 — Verify

  clawq channel test teams   # live OAuth round-trip to Azure AD
  clawq agent

## Session Key Format

```
teams:<team_id>:<conversation_id>
```

Sessions are shared per-channel: all users in the same Teams channel share one session. Sender identity is conveyed via context headers.

- Personal chats and group chats without a Teams channel context: `team_id` = `"personal"` (used when `channelData.team` is absent from the activity). Since `conversation_id` is unique per 1:1 chat, personal sessions are effectively per-user.
- Team channel messages: `team_id` = the `channelData.team.id` value from the activity.

## Authentication Details

### Inbound: JWT Claim Validation

Teams sends `Authorization: Bearer <jwt>` on every webhook POST.

Clawq validates:
- `aud` == your `app_id`
- `iss` == `"https://api.botframework.com"` or `"https://sts.windows.net/{tenant_id}/"`
- `exp` > current UTC time (not expired)
- `nbf` <= current UTC time + 5 min grace (not before)

**Known limitation**: RS256 signature verification (against Microsoft's JWKS) is not implemented — no RSA crypto library is available in clawq's dependency set. Claim-only validation is performed. This means a forged token with correct claims would pass. For higher-assurance deployments, put clawq behind a reverse proxy that performs full JWT signature verification.

### Outbound: OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials

Clawq fetches a bearer token before each reply batch:

```
POST https://login.microsoftonline.com/{tenant_id}/oauth2/v2.0/token
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

grant_type=client_credentials
&client_id={app_id}
&client_secret={app_secret}
&scope=https://api.botframework.com/.default
```

The token is cached until 60 seconds before its expiry (`expires_in - 60`).

## Message Size and Splitting

Maximum message size: **28,672 characters** per activity.

Replies longer than this limit are split at whitespace boundaries and sent as multiple sequential activities to the same conversation.

## How It Works (Request Flow)

1. Teams POSTs a JSON `Activity` to your webhook path.
2. Clawq's HTTP server returns **202 Accepted** immediately.
3. Asynchronously:
   a. JWT claims are verified.
   b. The activity is parsed: `type` must be `"message"`, `text` is extracted and stripped of `<at>BotName</at>` mention tags.
   c. `allow_teams` and `allow_users` filters are applied.
   d. Session key is constructed and the agent turn is dispatched via `Session.turn`.
   e. The reply is POSTed to `{serviceUrl}/v3/conversations/{conversation_id}/activities/{activity_id}`.

## Verifying the Integration

```bash
# Check channel config is loaded
clawq channel

# Verify daemon sees Teams
clawq agent &
# Should log: "Teams: webhook channel ready (handled via gateway HTTP server)"

# Smoke test with curl (replace with your actual values):
curl -X POST https://your-domain/teams/webhook \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer FAKE.JWT.TOKEN" \
  -d '{
    "type": "message",
    "id": "test-activity-id",
    "text": "Hello",
    "from": {"id": "user-aad-id", "name": "Test User"},
    "conversation": {"id": "conv-id", "isGroup": false},
    "serviceUrl": "https://smba.trafficmanager.net/amer/",
    "channelId": "msteams"
  }'
# Should return HTTP 202 {"status":"accepted"}
# (The JWT will fail claim validation — that is expected for fake tokens)
```

## Common Mistakes

- **Forgot the Teams App Manifest (Step 3)**: The bot exists in Azure but is invisible in Teams. This is the most common reason setup appears to work but the bot cannot be found.
- **Used http:// instead of https://**: Azure rejects non-HTTPS Messaging Endpoints.
- **Entered Messaging Endpoint before getting tunnel URL**: Update the endpoint in the Azure Bot resource after getting your real URL.
- **Copied app_id into app_secret**: The client secret is the value under Certificates & secrets, not the Application ID.
- **Client secret expired**: Rotate via Azure Portal → Certificates & secrets → New client secret.
- **Bot added to Teams channel in Azure but not installed in Teams**: The Teams channel in the Azure Bot Channels blade connects Azure to Teams, but you still need a Teams App Manifest to make the bot findable and installable.

## Troubleshooting

- **Auth failed** in logs: Check that `app_id` and `tenant_id` in config match your Azure app registration exactly.
- **Token fetch failed**: Verify `app_secret` is correct and not expired; check that the Azure AD app has the `https://api.botframework.com/.default` scope accessible.
- **No messages processed**: Ensure the webhook path is publicly reachable and the Messaging Endpoint in Azure Bot Service matches exactly (including path and https).
- **Reply not sent**: Check that `serviceUrl` in the activity is reachable from your server. The Bot Framework `serviceUrl` is region-specific and is provided per-activity; clawq uses the per-activity value if present.

## External References

- Bot Framework Activity schema: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bot-service/rest-api/bot-framework-rest-connector-api-reference
- Bot Framework authentication: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bot-service/rest-api/bot-framework-rest-connector-authentication
- Teams bot overview: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoftteams/platform/bots/what-are-bots
- Create a Teams bot: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/microsoftteams/platform/bots/how-to/create-a-bot-for-teams
- Register an AAD app: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/quickstart-register-app
- Azure Bot Service registration: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/bot-service/bot-service-quickstart-registration
- OAuth 2.0 client credentials (Microsoft identity): https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/active-directory/develop/v2-oauth2-client-creds-grant-flow
